stories.headline: Former Jeffco school psychologist sentenced to at least 5 years in prison for sexually assaulting student
stories.summary:
A Jefferson County District Court judge sentenced former Jeffco Public Schools psychologist James Michael Chevrier, 39, to an indeterminate prison term of five years to life this week for sexually assaulting a student. Chevrier worked as a psychologist at Green Mountain and Bear Creek high schools when he was arrested in May 2025 following a Safe2Tell report.

A jury convicted Chevrier in January of sexual assault on a child by one in a position of trust, two felony drug charges, and two misdemeanor counts of contributing to the delinquency of a minor. Prosecutors had sought the maximum 12-year sentence on the assault charge, arguing Chevrier exploited vulnerable students who came to him for help.

The victim spoke at Thursday's sentencing hearing, telling the court that Chevrier's actions robbed her of her freshman year of high school and her trust in adults. Deputy District Attorney Holly Spease said the abuse only stopped because the victim was brave enough to testify before a jury. The First Judicial District Attorney's Office previously confirmed the charges involved three students as well as separate drug-related crimes.

stories.headline: DUI driver strikes three cars on I-270, crashes into Commerce City home
stories.summary:
A driver struck three vehicles on Interstate 270 before careening off the highway and crashing into a residential home near 54th Avenue and Leyden Street in Commerce City late Friday night. The incident occurred around 11:40 p.m., according to the South Adams County Fire Department.

One occupant of the home and the driver both sustained minor injuries. The driver is facing multiple charges, including driving under the influence, police said.

The incident remains under investigation by authorities. No additional details about the driver's identity or the extent of structural damage to the home were immediately available.

stories.headline: Tornado watch issued for Colorado's Eastern Plains; severe thunderstorm warning covers parts of Adams and Arapahoe counties
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service issued a tornado watch for the northeastern corner of Colorado on Saturday, effective until 9 p.m., covering Cheyenne, Kit Carson, Lincoln, Logan, Phillips, Sedgwick, Washington and Yuma counties. The agency also issued a severe thunderstorm warning for portions of Adams, Arapahoe and Washington counties, citing a storm capable of producing ping pong ball-sized hail and winds up to 60 mph.

Nearly every county in Colorado was simultaneously under some form of severe weather watch or warning, with most communities facing red flag warnings for dangerous fire weather driven by high temperatures, low humidity and strong winds. The overlapping threats reflect an exceptionally active weather pattern across the state.

The dual hazards of fire weather and severe storms create significant challenges for emergency responders already stretched by active wildfire activity on the Western Slope and in the Front Range foothills. Residents on the Eastern Plains were urged to monitor conditions and have shelter plans in place.

stories.headline: Lightning-sparked Log Jumper Fire in southwest Douglas County 80% contained after brief evacuation warning
stories.summary:
A lightning-ignited wildfire in southwest Douglas County near the Devils Head Recreation Area prompted a brief evacuation warning Saturday before firefighters stopped its forward progress and authorities lifted the alert Saturday night. The Log Jumper Fire burned approximately one acre and reached 80% containment, according to the Douglas County Sheriff's Office and U.S. Forest Service updates.

The Douglas County Sheriff's Office initially issued an evacuation warning for a three-mile radius around the fire, located off Log Jumper Trail near Jackson Creek Road and Rampart Range Road, at 5:30 p.m. Several roads in the area were also closed, including South Platte River Road and South Highway 67/Sugar Creek Road.

U.S. Forest Service officials said crews would continue actively suppressing the fire overnight, with smoke expected to remain visible through Sunday morning. The fire is southwest of Castle Rock and sits within a broader statewide fire environment characterized by record-low snowpack, drought and extreme heat.

stories.headline: Rockies beat Pirates ace Paul Skenes 2-1 on bizarre interference call, clinch series win
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies claimed a 2-1 victory over the Pittsburgh Pirates on Saturday night at Coors Field before a crowd of 40,380, clinching the series on a game-ending baserunner interference call. It was Colorado's seventh series win of the season and their fourth in the last seven series.

The decisive moment came with the bases loaded and two outs in the ninth inning, when Pirates baserunner Billy Cook's cleat struck third baseman Kyle Karros's glove as Karros attempted to field a grounder. Umpires ruled Cook out for interference, ending the game. Pirates manager Don Kelly conceded after the game that the call was correct.

Leadoff hitter Jake McCarthy provided the game's biggest offensive play, clubbing an inside-the-park home run off Pirates ace Paul Skenes on the fourth pitch of the game — the first inside-the-park homer for the Rockies since Ian Desmond in 2019. Starter Tomoyuki Sugano outpitched Skenes over six innings, allowing one run on four hits with a season-high five strikeouts and no walks, earning his team-leading fourth quality start of the season.

stories.headline: Avalanche columnist argues Joe Sakic should trade Valeri Nichushkin as title window narrows
stories.summary:
With the Colorado Avalanche's championship window under pressure, Denver Post columnist Troy Renck argued this week that general manager Joe Sakic should trade winger Valeri Nichushkin, citing a sharp drop in the 31-year-old's postseason production. Since April 2025, Nichushkin has scored in just four of 19 playoff games and registered a point in six, compared to scoring in 12 of his previous 19 postseason appearances.

The statistical case is significant: the Avalanche are 12-14 in playoff games since spring 2025 when Nichushkin fails to score, compared to 8-4 when he does. Nichushkin carries a $6.125 million cap hit through the 2029-30 season, and the column notes that Cale Makar's upcoming contract extension will further strain Colorado's cap flexibility.

The column draws a parallel to the Nuggets' situation with Jamal Murray, noting both players appear to have lost the postseason production that fueled their respective franchises' title runs earlier this decade. Nichushkin scored just two goals in 12 playoff games during the 2026 postseason as Colorado was eliminated by Vegas.

stories.headline: MSU Denver men's basketball adds eight players, including Michael Porter Jr.'s brother, to 2026-27 roster
stories.summary:
MSU Denver men's basketball coach Dan Ficke announced the addition of eight players to the Roadrunners' 2026-27 roster, headlined by a mix of transfers and in-state freshmen. The incoming class includes transfers Elijah Mack, Justin Mack, Coban Porter and Mario Williams, alongside freshmen Hagen Hurst, Jeremiah Starr, Cole Tonniges and Cohen Edmundson.

Coban Porter, brother of former Nuggets forward Michael Porter Jr., previously played at the University of Denver before transferring to MSU Denver. Three of the freshman signees have Colorado roots: Jeremiah Starr from St. Mary's High School in Colorado Springs, Cole Tonniges from Mountain View High School in Loveland, and Cohen Edmundson from Lewis-Palmer High School in Monument.

Ficke said the class blends experienced transfers expected to make an immediate impact with energetic freshmen ready to develop. MSU Denver competes at the NCAA Division II level in the Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference.

stories.headline: Denver Gazette launches 'Colorado's 150 Cultural Shakers' series ahead of state sesquicentennial
stories.summary:
The Denver Gazette has launched a six-week series titled '150 Faces of Colorado Culture,' counting down 150 cultural figures who have shaped how Colorado sees itself and how the world sees Colorado, timed to the state's 150th birthday. The series prioritizes influence and legacy over fame, encompassing writers, performers, artists, architects and local legends.

The list spans high art and pop culture, household names and local icons, and is intentionally varied to reflect the breadth of Colorado's cultural identity. Notable figures previewed include musician Bob Dylan and Illenium, the globally recognized Denver-based electronic music artist. The series will run across six consecutive Sundays.

The inaugural entry spotlights Andrew Novick, a Denver artist, punk rocker and electrical engineer at the National Institute of Standards and Technology, whom the Gazette credits with more than three decades of cultural provocation in the city. Readers are encouraged to submit their own suggestions for the list.

stories.headline: Colorado wildfire conditions reach critical levels statewide amid drought, heat and high winds
stories.summary:
Colorado is facing an exceptionally dangerous fire season driven by record-low snowpack from the 2025-26 winter, widespread drought and a prolonged heat wave pushing Front Range temperatures into the upper 90s. The State Emergency Operations Center is activated at Response Level 3, and the Bureau of Land Management's Gunnison Field Office implemented Stage 1 Fire Restrictions on June 18 across six counties including Gunnison, Montrose and Ouray.

The National Weather Service issued Severe Red Flag Warnings for western Colorado, the San Luis Valley, Fremont County and multiple fire weather zones, with west winds of 15 to 25 mph and gusts to 45 mph and relative humidity dropping as low as 7 to 9 percent. The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment also issued Ozone Action Alerts for the Front Range as wildfire smoke and atmospheric stagnation contribute to unhealthy air quality.

The BLM is simultaneously conducting prescribed burns across approximately 2,285 acres in San Miguel, Dolores and La Plata counties to reduce hazardous fuels ahead of anticipated extreme fire behavior. Authorities emphasized that more than 90 percent of Colorado wildfires are human-caused and urged residents to avoid any spark-producing activity and prepare evacuation plans.

weather.notable: Nearly 8 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Chaos in the midst of chaos isn't funny, but chaos in the midst of order is.

topStory.headline: Vance Arrives in Switzerland as U.S.-Iran Nuclear Talks Begin Amid Hormuz Closure, Lebanon Fighting
topStory.summary:
Vice President JD Vance arrived at a Qatari-owned resort in Bürgenstock, Switzerland, early Sunday to lead the U.S. delegation in renewed negotiations with Iranian officials over Tehran's nuclear program and the ongoing conflict in Lebanon. The talks, mediated by Qatar and Pakistan, had originally been scheduled for Friday but were postponed, and Iran's Foreign Ministry said its delegation would meet with mediators in the morning before a four-way session with the U.S. in the afternoon. Pakistan's Ministry of Foreign Affairs described the gathering as 'technical-level talks.'

The negotiations opened under significant strain after Iran announced it was keeping the Strait of Hormuz closed, citing what it characterized as violations of an interim peace agreement reached earlier this week. Iran's top joint military command called the closure a 'first step' in response to alleged breaches by the U.S. and Israel, according to Iran's semiofficial Mehr news agency. Israeli strikes overnight Friday into Saturday killed at least 16 people in southern Lebanon, including two children, one day after the U.S. announced Israel and Hezbollah had implemented a fresh ceasefire at President Donald Trump's request.

The Iranian delegation is led by Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf and includes the deputy oil minister and the governor of Iran's central bank, reflecting Tehran's interest in securing sanctions relief and the unfreezing of Iranian assets held overseas. Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei described the trip as 'a mission to demand the fulfillment of the other side's obligations,' saying negotiations for a final agreement would begin only once those obligations were met. An internal dispute within Tehran has also complicated the talks, with some officials arguing that pursuing an agreement with the U.S. is futile.

Vance, speaking to reporters before departing, said the situation in Lebanon had 'calmed down' and expressed hope for progress on both the nuclear issue and the Lebanon ceasefire, noting he could remain in Switzerland for only 'a day or two.' Trump's special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are also participating in the negotiations. Trump separately threatened via social media to impose U.S. tolls in the Strait of Hormuz if a final deal with Iran is not reached within 60 days, the window established under the current interim agreement for toll-free passage.

The broader stakes are considerable. Trump has warned that without resolution of the strait issue, a global oil shortage and potential recession loom. The first clause of the memorandum of understanding published last week requires a ceasefire on all fronts, including Lebanon, but Israel was not a direct party to that agreement, leaving the U.S. in the difficult position of trying to restrain an ally while persuading Iran to continue talks. The thorniest unresolved issues — Iran's nuclear program, the scope of sanctions relief, and asset unfreezing — remain on the agenda for what the two sides have described as the formal start of 60 days of final negotiations.
